payload.input: More than three thousand species carve up the skies today, sorted into roughly thirty families that diverged while mammals still scurried beneath fern canopies. The largest are the darners, bodies like turquoise needles reaching three inches long, eyes that wrap around the head and touch at the top like clasped hands. These are the long-distance migrants, green darners crossing the Gulf of Mexico on wings that lock into gliding positions, their abdomens tapering to points that slice through humid air. Skimmers comprise the most diverse family, perchers that dominate pond edges with wings painted like stained glass—twelve-spotted skimmers bearing chocolate blotches on translucent membranes, Halloween pennants striped in orange and black, widow skimmers with wingtips dipped in ink. They hunt differently than their larger cousins, sallying from twigs to snatch prey rather than patrolling territories, their bodies often broader and flatter, built for quick turns rather than sustained speed. Clubtails carry a different architecture entirely, abdomens swelling at the tip into bulbs that resemble matchstick heads, these weighty counterbalances allowing them to hover over rushing streams where they hunt black flies. Their colors run to yellow and black tiger stripes, camouflage against sun-dappled rocks. Emeralds glitter with metallic green thoraxes that shift to bronze depending on light angle, nymphs that prefer cold mountain seeps and bogs, adults that patrol forest clearings with a hovering motion distinct from the darting of low-fliers. At the ancient end of the spectrum, petaltails survive as living fossils in Australian rainforests and Japanese mountains, wings shaped like elongated petals, nymphs that burrow into stream banks and breathe through telescoping rectal chambers. The smallest measure barely an inch, scarlet dwarfs with thread-thin abdomens, while the giant petaltail of Japan reaches five inches, a creature that looks unchanged from Cretaceous fossils.
